data.title: Growth At A Reasonable Price: The Case For GARP Investing
data.description:
By Priya Panse, CFA; Eric Legunn, CFA

What is GARP investing?

Growth stocks have significantly outperformed value stocks over the past 20 years.1 Investors concerned that the trend may have gone too far, but do not want to completely abandon growth investing may want to consider a hybrid approach, known as GARP, or Growth at a Reasonable Price.

GARP seeks to help investors maintain growth exposure - especially in exciting areas like AI - while aiming to not overpay for growth (hence the acronym) by considering valuation levels.

GARP is a hybrid stock-picking style combining growth and value investing. The goal is to find companies with strong earnings growth prospects that aren’t trading at excessive valuations. GARP screens first for growth stocks, then tilts the portfolio toward those with attractive value and quality characteristics.

The iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP), which rose approximately 37% in 2024 and has gained over 22% year-to-date through October 31, 20252, seeks to help investors maintain growth exposure - while supporting thoughtful security selection that considers valuation and concentration risk.

Growth vs. Value: Outperformance and Stretched Valuations

Over the past 20 years, growth stocks have dramatically outperformed value stocks, with a cumulative performance differential of more than 730%.

Russell 1000 Value vs. Russell 1000 Growth, last 20 years

Source: FactSet, as of 9/30/2025. The last 20 years of monthly total return data are shown for the Russell 1000 Growth Total Return Index and the Russell 1000 Value Total Return Index. For comparison purposes, the indices are scaled to 100 on 9/30/2005. Index performance is for illustrative purposes only. Index performance does not reflect any management fees or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Chart description: Line chart showing the outperformance of the Russell 1000 Growth Total Return Index vs. the Russell 1000 Value Total Return Index for the past 20 years.

However, this outperformance has come at a cost: growth exhibits loftier market valuations relative to its own history than value.

Growth valuations are stretched relative to history, more so than for Value

Source: FactSet, BlackRock Calculation. Annual data, as of 12/31/2024. Trailing twelve-month P/E ratios (TTM P/E) for the iShares Russell 1000 growth ETF (IWF) and the iShares Russell 1000 value ETF (IWD), are shown over the last 25 years.

Chart description: Line chart showing how the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF) trades 48% above its historic average PE compared to the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), which trades 17% above its historic average.

At the end of 2024, the price-to-earnings (PE) ratio for growth traded at a 48% premium relative to its historical average, nearly three times higher premium than for value, which exhibited a smaller 17% premium relative to its historical average.3 Investors concerned with such lofty valuation levels, but who do not want to abandon growth investing completely, may consider pursuing GARP investing.

GARP investing allows investors to maintain exposure to high-growth potential names while being mindful of valuation risk. The iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP), for example, offers a hybrid solution that sits between growth and value — seeking growth as a primary objective but tilting toward value and quality characteristics.

As a result, GARP’s portfolio has exhibited lower valuation levels than comparable growth indices, while still higher than the market4 itself, given the growth focus.

Valuation Comparison: Russell 1000 vs. GARP vs. Russell 1000 Growth

Source: BlackRock Calculation, FactSet, as of 9/30/2025. Data for the Russell 1000 ETF, GARP ETF, and Russell 1000 Growth ETF is sourced from FactSet Market Aggregates last twelve-month price-to-earnings, last twelve-month price-to-book, last twelve-month price-to-sales, and last twelve-month price-to-cash-flow ratios for the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP), and iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF) respectively.

Chart description: Bar chart showing a valuation comparison of the Russell 1000 ETF vs. the iShares GARP ETF vs. the iShares Russell Growth ETF based on price-to-earnings, price-to-book, price-to-sales, and price-to-cashflow.

GARP still looks like a growth exposure when it comes to a sector perspective. Sector deviations relative to a typical market-cap-weighted growth exposure, such as the Russell 1000 Growth Index, have been modest given GARP’s primary objective of delivering a growth exposure.

Sector Exposure: iShares MSCl USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP) vs. iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF)

Source: BlackRock, Aladdin, as of 9/26/2025.

Chart description: Bar chart showing the sector exposure differences of the iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF vs. the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF.

The result is a portfolio with similar sector exposure relative to the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF, but larger differences when it comes to security selection within sectors.

One notable example is how GARP has reduced concentration risk in the so-called Magnificent Seven stocks: Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

Consider the weight of the Magnificent Seven names in the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), and iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP).

Magnificent 7 exposure

Source: Morningstar Direct, as of 9/30/2025.

Chart description: Bar chart showing the weight of the "Magnificent Seven" names in the iShares Russell 1000 ETF vs. the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF, and the iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF.

As illustrated above, while GARP significantly reduces exposure to the Magnificent Seven, it does not eliminate them. In fact, the only Magnificent Seven stock that GARP fully excludes per its methodology, which considers value and quality characteristics in addition to growth, is Tesla.5

We view that still having nearly 25% of the fund’s overall stock exposure in the Magnificent Seven is an important feature of GARP, since they have been key market-driving names.

(Specific companies or issuers are mentioned for educational purposes only and should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities. Any companies mentioned do not necessarily represent current or future holdings of any BlackRock products. (For actual fund holdings, please visit the respective fund product pages.)

Conclusion

We believe GARP’s blend of growth and value is especially timely given the Fed’s recent pivot to rate cuts and the possibility that the economy may begin to slow. GARP seeks both offensive and defensive characteristics by focusing on companies with strong fundamentals, more reasonable valuations, and quality metrics.

In portfolios, GARP can potentially complement core holdings, diversify existing growth exposures, and potentially reduce concentration risk without sacrificing exposure to key growth themes in today’s markets.

data.title: Hartford Checks And Balances Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Geopolitics, trade policy, and monetary signals dominated markets during the third quarter. NATO's warning to Russia over airspace violations kept geopolitical risk elevated, while US government shutdown threats added domestic uncertainty. Trade policy developments were mixed: new tariffs on timber and lumber and broader tariff threats contrasted with incremental progress on negotiations, leaving global supply chains and inflation expectations in focus. The Federal Reserve cut rates for the first time since late 2024, citing labor market softness, and signaled openness to further easing if conditions deteriorate. Inflation remained near 3%, reinforcing expectations for a gradual policy shift. These factors, combined with currency volatility and fluctuating energy prices, shaped investor positioning across regions and styles.

Equities led performance for the quarter, with US large caps ahead of developed international markets, while emerging markets outpaced both, benefiting from improving trade sentiment and a weaker dollar. Growth styles generally held an advantage over value, though leadership rotated as investors weighed technology optimism against cyclical recovery themes. Fixed income delivered positive returns but lagged equities, as falling Treasury yields supported core bonds and tighter credit spreads 1 boosted corporate debt. High yield and emerging market debt outperformed investment-grade bonds, reflecting risk-on sentiment despite lingering macro uncertainty. Overall, the quarter highlighted the interplay between policy shifts, geopolitical developments, and sector rotation, reinforcing the importance of diversified positioning across asset classes.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Checks and Balances Fund (I Share) underperformed its blended benchmark and the Lipper Mixed-Asset Target Allocation Growth peer group average in the third quarter.
  • Under performance versus the benchmark was due to performance of the Underlying Funds as Hartford Dividend and Growth Fund lagged its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, and Hartford Capital Appreciation Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000 Index. The Hartford Total Return Bond ETF outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index.

Positioning & Outlook

  • We recognize that inflation is trending lower but remains sticky in services. Global policy easing supports risk assets, yet the margin for error is tight given high valuations and persistent macro risks, including fiscal deficits and potential rate shocks.
  • We remain constructive on equities, with US stocks regaining leadership as AI-driven earnings growth continues. However, we are mindful of elevated valuations and risks from tariffs, trade tensions, and a weakening labor market.
  • At the end of the period, our largest equity overweights were to Financials and Energy, while our largest underweights were to Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary relative to the equity component of the blended benchmark.
  • Within fixed income, the portfolio maintains an underweight to investment-grade credit due to better observed opportunities in various out-of-benchmark sectors including high-quality securitized credit and select emerging markets. The portfolio is also overweight agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS). At quarter-end, the portfolio duration 2 was moderately longer than the benchmark.

data.title: BNY Mellon New York AMT-Free Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Municipal bonds rallied alongside U.S. Treasuries in 3Q25, spurred by the Federal Reserve's first rate cut of the year and markets pricing in an additional one to two 25 bp reductions by year-end. The Bloomberg Municipal Index (tax-exempt) outperformed both Treasury and corporate benchmarks in the quarter—reversing its year-to-date lag—thanks to an improving supply/demand backdrop. Demand from individuals remained solid, with mutual funds and ETFs attracting over $10 billion and $25 billion of inflows, respectively, year-to-date. Looking ahead the potential for a further rate rally could support additional asset-class inflows. Primary issuance at $433 billion year-to-date (up 12 percent year-over-year) is on record annual pace. Supply typically peaks in October before tapering into year-end as tax-exempt reinvestment flows and seasonal demand firm the technical backdrop. Municipal yields remain elevated by historical standards and are attractive on a taxable-equivalent basis (TEY), with the Bloomberg Muni Index's TEY ending the third-quarter at 6.2 percent.

"Uncertain changes to federal policy could drive heightened volatility in credit conditions as we move through 2025."

Municipal credit remains robust, underpinned by the large reserves and cash balances that issuers have built up in recent years. Those strength factors helped drive rating upgrades for both New Jersey and Connecticut in the second quarter. However, several executive actions by the current administration—if allowed to stand—could create adverse credit pressures across broad segments of public finance. Early signs suggest that tariffs, federal workforce reductions, & mass deportations are slowing economic growth, which may weigh on state & local income & sales tax revenues.

From a sector standpoint, we favor revenue-producing areas like public power and water/sewer utilities, which benefit from independent rate-setting, essential services, and relatively predictable cash flows. Conversely, we are under-weight in sectors with lower yield premiums—such as state and local general-obligation bonds and pre-refunded issues. We remain cautious on hospital bonds amid margin pressures and anticipated Medicaid cuts, with a tilt toward larger health-system issuers in growth markets. Above all, we emphasize geographic and sector diversification while targeting issuers with resilient balance sheets and strong fiscal flexibility.

Federal policy shifts under the current administration have introduced some uncertainty into credit conditions, but most municipal sectors remain well-positioned to absorb any headwinds. Through August 15, 2025, rating agency activity shows upgrades outnumbering downgrades in nearly every sector—Higher Education and Water/Sewer being the primary exceptions. In this climate, a more cautious credit selection framework is advisable, with heightened focus on issuers' balance-sheet strength, liquidity and operational flexibility. And while 2025 has seen limited hurricane activity to date, we continue to stress the importance of embedding climate risk analysis into the investment process.

The US economy is slowing, and while we maintain a “no recession” base view, the outlook is increasingly vulnerable to downside risks as the effects of tight policy via tariffs and immigration crackdowns continue to reverberate across the economy.

While the Fed may still be concerned with the full inflationary effects of tariffs, rising labor market stress should bias the Fed toward more aggressive easing throughout the rest of the year and into 2026. We maintain a duration stance that is longer versus our benchmarks. Year-to-date, bonds maturing beyond ten years have underperformed shorter maturities—steepening the yield curve and creating compelling value out toward the long-end. We believe this segment can produce attractive relative returns as demand is likely to pick up under a more accommodative Federal Reserve. A barbell curve posture may be favorable with exposure to short maturities which should benefit from further Fed easing while longer maturity bonds offer attractive yield in a relatively steep curve environment.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the Fund's Class I shares returned 3.10%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund's unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Municipal Bond Index, returned 3.00% for the same period.

Performance Review

The Fund's long duration profile supported positive performance but was partially offset by an overweight to longer term maturities, which underperformed as the front end of the yield curve experienced a stronger rally. Security selection was additive with issues in education, special tax, and water and sewer having the best relative performance. Decisions to underweight leasing, housing, and gas sectors modestly underperformed.

Market Outlook

Elevated financial market volatility is likely to continue amid ongoing uncertainty around tariffs, geopolitical alliances, and overall policy change prospects tied to federal funding, among others. We remain vigilant with maintaining well diversified, high-quality portfolios consisting of solid fundamental credits that we believe are well-positioned to withstand pressures from potential policy changes in Washington. We also would view any market dislocation stemming from federal policy uncertainty as a potential buying opportunity.

Uncertain changes to federal policy could drive heightened volatility in credit conditions as we move through 2025. We believe a more cautious approach to credit selection would be prudent, with an increased emphasis on balance sheet strength, liquidity and operating flexibility. Essential service sectors, including public power and water/sewer, remain relatively safe investments due to their predictable and stable cash flows through economic cycles. Healthcare is facing potential headwinds stemming from potential cuts to Medicaid, which could have an outsized negative impact on smaller, rural hospitals. Threatened cuts to federal grants and potential changes to endowment tax rates are driving negative headlines among many of the higher rated colleges and universities, yet we believe these institutions have substantial resources to withstand the cuts and would view any spread widening as a buying opportunity. We continue to see value in the transportation sectors although airports with heavy reliance on international travel are likely to see some softening in demand.

Our duration posture is targeted to be modestly longer versus respective benchmarks. The recent backup in municipal yields along with yield curve steepening has made the current environment more attractive for modest duration extension. The prospect for transitory impacts of higher inflation from fiscal and tariff policy from the new administration along with the Fed's dovish bias may keep the slope of the curve elevated and rangebound in the near term.

Reflects investments of dividends and, where applicable, capital gain distributions. The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the US Treasury. Treasury bills are excluded by the maturity constraint but are part of a separate Short Treasury Index. The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index measures the performance of the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market within the United States. The Bloomberg Municipal Managed Money Long Index is a rules-based, market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of publicly traded, USD-denominated, long-term, tax-exempt municipal bonds, including general obligation bonds, revenue bonds, insured bonds, and pre-refunded bonds. The Bloomberg 3-Year Municipal Bond Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance

of a specific segment of the municipal bond market. It includes fixed-rate, investment-grade, tax-exempt bonds with remaining maturities between two and four years. The Bloomberg 1-10 Year Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of investment-grade municipal bonds with maturities between 1 and 10 years. Bloomberg Municipal Bond AAA, AA, A and BBB Indices are subsets of the Bloomberg Barclays Municipal Bond Index. The Bloomberg Municipal High Yield Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of the non-investment grade, tax-exempt municipal bond market in the United States. It includes bonds that are rated below investment grade (Ba1 or lower) or are unrated and are U.S. dollar-denominated and fixed-rate. The Bloomberg Municipal Taxable Bonds Index measures the performance of the U.S. municipal taxable investment-grade bond market. It includes bonds issued by state and local governments that are subject to federal income tax. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve predicts the direction of interest rates and the economic expansion or contraction that could result.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond or other debt instrument to a change in interest rates. A bond's duration is easily confused with its term or time to maturity because they are both measured in years. However, a bond's term is a linear measure of the years until repayment of principal is due; it does not change with the interest rate environment. Duration, on the other hand is non-linear and accelerates as time to maturity lessens. Dovish describes a monetary policy stance that favors lower interest rates to stimulate economic growth and potentially increase employment, even if it means accepting a higher level of inflation. The tax-equivalent yield (TEY) is the return a taxable bond needs to equal the yield on a comparable tax-exempt municipal bond. Investors use this calculation to compare the returns between a tax-free investment and a taxable alternative.

data.title: Touchstone Strategic Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Recap

The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered its first rate cut of the year in September. Softening employment reports, including negative revisions, provided evidence of downside risk to the labor market which prompted the Fed to engage in a 25-basis point “risk management” cut. In addition, the Fed adjusted their expectations for the path of rates, forecasting two more cuts this year and one in 2026 in spite of slight increases in growth and inflation forecasts. This rate adjustment and forward guidance continue to ease financial conditions for the economy despite lingering uncertainty.

Trade policy continues to be a primary source of uncertainty for investors. While the White House has announced a handful of deals and frameworks for deals, there are still a lot of details that remain unknown, especially related to China. In addition to tariff mechanics, questions remain around the legality of certain tariffs that utilized International Emergency Economic Powers Act authority. The U.S. Trade Court ruled these tariffs illegal, but the White House appeal sent the case to the Supreme Court which agreed to hear oral arguments in early November.

While economic growth has slowed since last year, expectations for 2026 have started to improve. Full expensing for manufacturing structures, research & development, and equipment investment was made law by the Big, Beautiful Bill in July, which should boost capital expenditure over the coming quarters. Consumer spending remains largely resilient, driven by higher income cohorts. However, weaker employment data shows that the economy is just marginally adding jobs, presenting an environment that could cause a rise in the unemployment rate.

Despite ongoing trade, geopolitical and policy uncertainties, the S&P 500 reached new highs. Credit spreads tightened beyond historical averages as expectations for rate cuts rose and long-term inflation remained subdued, driving longer yields lower.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Strategic Income Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.

Sector allocation was the largest contributor to relative outperformance due primarily to the overweight allocation to high yield corporates, emerging market debt, and non-credit sectors. These sectors outperformed as spreads tightened and risk assets moved higher over the period.

Interest rate positioning detracted modestly from relative performance during the quarter. Interest rates declined over the quarter and the curve steepened as investors increased their expectations for rate cuts. While the Fund utilized tactical duration management, the Fund was managed with shorter duration than the benchmark which detracted from performance as interest rates moved lower.

Security selection was a slight detractor from relative outperformance due to underperformance in emerging market debt and securitized which was only partially offset by outperformance within investment-grade corporates. Within securitized, an underweight to agency passthroughs detracted from relative performance. Underperformance within emerging market debt was due to an overweight position in Latin American corporates.

The Fund did not materially adjust sector allocations over the quarter as we maintained a focus on finding issuers with compelling bottom-up narratives. However, the Fund was positioned with less duration than the benchmark and averaged 5.1 years over the quarter, compared to 6.1 for the benchmark. Interest rates declined over the quarter as investors increased their expectations for rate cuts amid labor market weakness. While the Fund tactically adjusted duration positioning, partially offsetting the negative impact of interest rate positioning, interest rate management detracted from relative performance as the Fund was managed shorter than the benchmark as interest rates declined.

Outlook and Conclusion

Current valuations are back to levels that are tight relative to history. Incorporating our macroeconomic outlook and elevated uncertainty, the Fund is positioned with a modest overweight risk posture and an ability to add risk as opportunities arise. Sector positioning reflects current valuations, relative value, and opportunities within each sector. The Fund’s notable change to sector allocations was the increase to high yield corporates in April, and subsequent reduction in Treasuries. Other sector allocations were mostly unchanged during the quarter.

Exposure to investment-grade credit was maintained over the quarter. The sector continues to favor positions lower in the capital structure within high-quality financials and is selectively adding to bottom-up opportunities on attractive relative value, including targeting investments at the front and intermediate part of the curve. The Fund’s allocation to securitized products was largely unchanged over the quarter. We continue to favor non-agency exposure and are overweight risk within asset-backed securities, collateralized loan obligations, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. In addition, the Fund added marginally to discount non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities given attractive upside return potential. The Fund maintained its exposure to emerging markets debt which is toward the lower end of the Fund’s historical range. The sector is primarily invested in compelling bottom-up narratives within the below-investment-grade segment. Latin America is the largest exposure within the sector.

High yield exposure was increased in April due to attractive relative value as spreads widened following tariff announcements. However, spreads are back to levels that are tight relative to history and the Fund is opportunistically reducing exposure. The high-yield allocation is broadly diversified by sector, favoring quality, and is positioned for an opportunity to add more risk at more attractive spreads.

The yield curve steepened over the quarter, and the Fund is targeting a largely neutral curve positioning. However, we expect further opportunities for tactical adjustments as volatility remains elevated and investors adjust their expectations for future rate cuts amid new economic data.

The Fund is positioned to navigate the current environment of elevated uncertainty and more moderate economic growth. We believe a modest overweight to credit sectors is prudent given valuations are tight of historical medians and uncertainty exists from trade policy, geopolitical tension, and pending court decisions on executive authority. While growth forecasts for 2026 have been slowly improving, recent labor market weakness presents downside risk to economic growth. If the economic landscape remains stable, the Fund is positioned to benefit from additional spread risk relative to the benchmark. However, if economic growth slows materially, the Fund is positioned to add exposure opportunistically, as risk assets would likely experience further weakness. Additionally, we believe positive security selection can benefit the Fund across different market environments and tactical duration management allows the Fund to take advantage of elevated interest rate volatility.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in fixed-income securities which can experience reduced liquidity during certain market events, lose their value as interest rates rise and are subject to credit risk which is the risk of deterioration in the financial condition of an issuer and/or general economic conditions that can cause the issuer to not make timely payments of principal and interest also causing the securities to decline in value and an investor can lose principal. When interest rates rise, the price of debt securities generally falls. Longer-term securities are generally more volatile. The Fund invests in non-investment-debt securities which are considered speculative with respect to the issuers' ability to make timely payments of interest and principal, may lack liquidity and has had more frequent and larger price changes than other debt securities. The Fund invests in mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities which are subject to the risks of prepayment, defaults, changing interest rates and at times, the financial condition of the issuer. The Fund invests in investment-grade debt securities which may be downgraded by an NRSRO to below investment-grade status. The Fund invests in U.S. government agency securities which are neither issued nor guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury and are not guaranteed against price movements due to changing interest rates. The Advisor engages a sub-advisor to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-advisor who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisors. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in preferred stocks which are relegated below bonds for payment should the issuer be liquidated. If interest rates rise, the fixed dividend on preferred stocks may be less attractive, causing their price to decline. The Fund invests in foreign securities which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund invests in emerging markets securities which are more likely to experience turmoil or rapid changes in market or economic conditions than developed countries. The Fund invests in sovereign debt securities which are issued by foreign governments whose respective economies could have an important effect on their ability or willingness to service their debt which could affect the value of the securities. The Fund invests in derivatives such as futures contracts. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid and difficult to value, subject to counterparty and leverage risks and there is risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the Fund will not correlate with the Fund’s other investments. Gains or losses from speculative positions in a derivative may be much greater than the original cost and potential losses may be substantial. The Fund invests in mortgage dollar rolls which involve increased risk and volatility, as the securities the Fund is required to repurchase may be worth less than the securities that the Fund originally held. The Fund may experience higher portfolio turnover which may lead to increased fund expenses, lower investment returns and higher short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders. The Fund invests in repurchase agreements which are considered loans by the Fund and may suffer a loss of principal and interest in the event of counterparty defaults. data.title: Fidelity Overseas Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the three months ending September 30, 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 0.12%, notably trailing the 4.80% advance of the benchmark MSCI EAFE Index (Net MA).

International developed-market equities rallied in the third quarter, but the gains were less robust than in Q2. Factors providing a tailwind for the group included the continued generative AI boom, strong corporate earnings, and an interest-rate cut by the U.S. Federal Reserve in September. While concerns about U.S. trade policy were not as dominant as they had been earlier in the year, and progress was made on several fronts, investor uncertainty persisted. Companies around the globe were reconfiguring their supply chains to reduce their dependence on the U.S. and China, the two nations at the center of recent trade conflict.

Looking at the major regions within the benchmark, Japan led the way (+8%). Although currency volatility and policy uncertainty were intermittent challenges, confidence in an earnings recovery, coupled with Japan's structural reform momentum, remained critical performance drivers. The U.K. and Asia Pacific ex Japan region (+6% each) also fared well in Q3, whereas Europe ex U.K. (+3%), which had done well the previous quarter, underperformed. More broadly, value outperformed growth by a considerable margin the past three months. This proved to be a strong performance headwind for the fund. While we certainly pay attention to valuations, we also look for dependable, high-quality growth, which was not in favor this quarter.

Within the portfolio, positioning in Europe ex U.K. was by far the most significant relative detractor on a geographic basis, especially in Germany and the Netherlands. Picks in the U.K. and, Canada, along with unfavorable exposure to Japan, hurt as well. Among sectors, investment choices in financial and industrials notably weighed on the fund's relative outcome in Q3.

On a stock-specific basis, an out-of-benchmark position in Constellation Software (-26%) detracted the most. In August, the Toronto-headquartered provider of software solutions reported lower earnings and revenue for the second quarter, but financial results were largely in line with or slightly better than analysts' expectations. The company announced several acquisitions during the quarter, and in late September said its founder and president resigned immediately from his position for health reasons. We modestly pared the stock this period.

The portfolio's outsized exposure to Deutsche Börse (-18%) disappointed us as well. The Germany stock exchange and financial data provider had done quite well in the first half of 2025 amid excitement about the government's plans to ramp up spending on infrastructure and defense, and, in our view, might have been due for some consolidation in Q3.

An overweight stake in DSV also proved detrimental to performance. Shares of the firm returned about -17% in the third quarter, sliding in late July after the world's second-largest transport and logistics provider reported Q2 earnings that narrowly missed consensus estimates. The shortfall was primarily due to weakness in DSV's Road and Contract Logistics businesses, while its key Air & Sea division performed largely in line with expectations, supported by the recent acquisition of DB Schenker.

Conversely, positioning in Spain bolstered the fund's performance versus the benchmark this past quarter. Non-benchmark exposure to China and Taiwan also helped. By sector, an underweight allocation to consumer staples stocks stood out to the upside.

A larger-than-benchmark position in British multinational aerospace and defense company Rolls-Royce Holdings (+21%) meaningfully contributed the past three months. In late July, the company reported better-than-expected revenue and notably improved profitability for the first half of 2025. All three of the firm's core divisions – Civil Aerospace, Defense, and Power Systems – contributed to results, with the latter faring particularly well, boosted by rising demand for data-center backup electric power. The stock has advanced amid an ongoing rally for Europe-based defense businesses, driven by investors' expectation of a meaningful ramp-up in military spending in the U.K. and European Union in the next several years, along with a post-pandemic recovery in air travel propelling demand for widebody jet engines. Despite trimming our stake somewhat, Rolls-Royce was the portfolio's second-largest holding on September 30.

A non-index allocation to China-based Tencent Holdings (+29%) also helped, supported by the company's better-than-expected earnings in the second quarter. In August, management highlighted that Q2 revenue increased 15%, driven largely by strength in its core international online gaming business, as well as AI-related upgrades across all business lines. The firm also reported that revenue in its fintech & business services segment ticked up 10%, which exceeded analysts' expectations. Tencent was a newly established holding this past quarter.

Outlook and Positioning

We struggle to recall a time when fundamental investors have been forced to contend with as much as they have so far in 2025. The lingering uncertainty and tariff volatility is likely already having a negative impact on the global economy, as CEOs and consumers pause large investments. In addition, we have fiscal austerity in the U.S. and the exact opposite in Europe, with Germany unleashing a wave of defense and infrastructure spending.

With this heightened uncertainty and divergent fiscal and monetary policies, we are focusing on parts of the equity market where either future earnings power or shareholder distributions are within a company's control, to a large extent. For example, one area of emphasis for the fund is European defense firms, given the fact that there is roughly 10 years of earnings visibility for these businesses. However, this trade is no secret, and consequently, valuations are on the high side. As a result, we plan to vary the portfolio's exposure to this segment of the market according to how valuations are looking.

The fund's top geographic overweights at the end of September were the U.K., Spain and Germany. Prominent British holdings of note included Rolls-Royce Holdings, 3i Group (OTCPK:TGOPF), Compass Group (OTCPK:CMPGY) and Linde (LIN). Additionally, the fund had out-of-benchmark stakes of roughly 4% and 3%, respectively, in the U.S. and Canada as of quarter end.

In contrast, the portfolio continued to maintain comparatively light exposure to Japan, Switzerland and the Netherlands, while avoiding Australia altogether. In Japan, notable underweights at the conclusion of the period included Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG), Toyota Motor (TM), Sony Group (SONY) and SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY), all of which we chose to avoid because we considered them overvalued versus their earnings-growth prospects. Key underweights in Australia included Commonwealth Bank of Australia (OTCPK:CMWAY) and BHP Group (BHP).

Turning to sectors, the portfolio's outsized exposure to industrials, financials and information technology was noteworthy at the end of September. On the other hand, the fund carried sizable underweights in health care, consumer discretionary and consumer staples, while avoiding energy and real estate entirely at period end. As always, these weightings were arrived at by bottom-up, fundamental analysis, not top-down decisions. Within industrials, we continued to emphasize the capital goods and commercial & professional services segments. In technology, semiconductors & semiconductor equipment stocks represented the largest industry overweight on September 30.

Overall, the fund's three largest individual overweights at quarter end were Safran, Banco Santander and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, whereas the most prominent underweights included HSBC Holdings, Roche Holding and Novartis, none of which held in the portfolio at quarter end.

Fidelity® Overseas Fund (FOSFX)

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Overseas Fund is a diversified international equity strategy that seeks long-term capital growth.

The fund's philosophy centers around owning high-quality companies purchased at attractive prices that can outperform the market over the long term, given their ability to compound value through the re-investment of earnings back into the business at attractive returns. By focusing on buying these companies at inexpensive valuations, we attempt to build in an inherent margin of safety in the investment.

We particularly favor firms with unique business models that are able to generate sustainably high returns on capital through a full business cycle and are trading at a discount to their intrinsic (fair) value based on several conservative valuation frameworks.

We strive to uncover these companies through in-depth fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team, as well as by leveraging the fund manager's own independent analysis.

data.title: CECO Environmental: Impressive Business Momentum, Too Much Share Momentum
data.description:
In May, I concluded that the market was awaiting margin improvements in the case of CECO Environmental (CECO). The business saw strong sales growth, yet margins remained volatile, as that latter observation made me a bit cautious on the shares.

That conclusion has been far too conservative with the benefit of hindsight, as shares have doubled in the half a year ever since, now trading around the $52 mark. While orders and sales are growing spectacularly and margins are improving, I just cannot escape the feeling that the move has been overextended.

A Leading and Global Industrial Environmental Solutions Business

The paragraph header is how CECO describes its own business, which was founded in 1966. The company has a mission to protect people, the environment, and industrial equipment.

The company focuses 100% on industrial clients, these including general industrial companies, beverage can producers, food & beverage producers, waste and wastewater providers, semiconductors, renewable technology providers, data center operators, natural gas infrastructure providers, among others.

Energy transition products make up about 40% of sales, these including emission management, thermal & acoustic management, combustion management, etc. Industrial air makes up 35% of sales, with solutions and products provided including silencers, scrubbers, dust removals, etc. Industrial water makes up the remaining quarter of sales, this including supply water and wastewater solutions, and others.

While the business has been active in these markets for many decades, the performance in the past was rather uneven, to put it mildly. After all, a $15 stock in 2013 traded at just $5 in 2022, but ever since was unleashed following a period of a real strategic transition, complemented by targeted M&A efforts.

A $350 million revenue base in 2015 rose to just over $400 million in 2022, these revenues accompanied by single-digit operating margins, with modest dilution incurred over this period of time.

Momentum Accelerates

The momentum for CECO arrived in 2022, a year in which revenues rose by 30% to $422 million, while orders rose some 46% to $526 million, with adjusted earnings tripling to $0.76 per share.

While the strategic actions looked to be a solid explanation for the momentum observed, the strength felt a bit unexpected and surprising, as sales rose by another 29% to $545 million in 2023. During 2024, the company announced a $125 million acquisition of Profire Energy, with this company adding $60 million in annual sales. Moreover, the company announced the purchase of Verantis as well, adding another $45 million in sales.

Both these deals only closed early in 2025, making that 2024 revenues were up just 2% to $558 million, as the backlog rose another 14% to $667 million. Adjusted EBITDA was up 9% to $63 million, with adjusted earnings flattish at $0.75 per share, these earnings being quite adjusted.

Many Moving Targets

Following the release of the 2024 results, the company guided for 2025 sales to move up to $700-$750 million, with EBITDA seen at $90-$100 million, aided by M&A moves, as the company announced the divestment of its fluid handling business in April. This deal was valued at $110 million.

First quarter sales rose by 40% to $177 million, as the order intake was reported at $228 million, up 57% on the year, adding to a backlog that exceeds $600 million. While that was really impressive, adjusted operating income was down 16% to just $8.6 million, with adjusted earnings down a penny to $0.10 per share.

However, these softer earnings were achieved on the back of a $14 million adjusted EBITDA number, which annualised came in far short compared to the $90-$100 million guidance for the year. With 37 million shares trading at $25 and net debt seen just shy of $200 million, the enterprise valuation of $1.1 billion looked very reasonable in relation to a $700 million sales guidance, equal to around 1.5 times sales.

However, the issue was that of margins, as I only saw full-year adjusted earnings around a dollar per share based on the guidance, with real volatility expected around tariffs, which made it hard to build conviction around the $25 mark.

On Fire

Other than the regular quarterly results, it has been rather quiet on the corporate front this year. Second quarter sales rose by 35% to $185 million, as a 95% increase in the order intake to $274 million was outright spectacular. These advancements resulted in some operating leverage, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $23 million and change, with adjusted earnings reported at $0.24 per share.

Following the sales momentum, the full-year sales guidance was hiked by twenty-five million to $725-$775 million, while the EBITDA guidance was maintained.

Third-quarter results were released by the end of October. Revenues rose by 46% to $198 million, with orders up 44% to $233 million, adding to a $720 million backlog. EBITDA was reported at $23 million, equal to the second quarter, with adjusted earnings reported at $0.26 per share; these numbers look quite clean.

The company maintained the guidance on all fronts for 2025, as the company offered an upbeat guidance for 2026. Revenues are seen at $850-$950 million next year, with EBITDA seen up to $110-$130 million. This suggests that some real momentum could be seen as earnings trend around a dollar per share now.

The outlook calls for a $30 million incremental EBITDA contribution, which, given a low depreciation intensity of the business, could make that pre-tax profits per share could jump by about eighty cents based on a share tally of 36 million shares. This could create a realistic roadmap for earnings to move up to $1.50 per share next year. In either case, expectations have risen on this front to a mid-thirty times earnings multiple based on such projections for 2026 earnings.

By now, the 36 million shares are valued at just over $1.8 billion, for a $2.0 billion enterprise valuation based on a stable net debt load of $188 million. This values the business at around 2-2.5 times sales, as EBITDA multiples are moving up to the high teens, with both sales and margins moving in the right direction.

And Now?

Part of the re-rating is certainly deserved, as momentum is simply very strong after lots of self-help, which is to be applauded. However, real earnings power is still lagging a bit, as further margin gains could provide more support to the investment case here.

What is clear is that CECO is no longer the same business as it was in the past, aided by strong order gains and real growth, although the heavy work now has to come from further margin gains. I have no concerns on the topline, certainly not as 2026 orders are seen topping a billion.

While EBITDA margins are seen up nearly a point to 13% and change in 2026, I have taken note that free cash flow conversion from this EBITDA number is seen only at 50-60% next year, compared to a 60% target this year.

Given all this, I congratulate the business a lot but have concerns about the realistic margins, which would not be an issue if not for the fact that shares doubled so far this year. This makes me naturally a bit cautious here, and while I applaud the achievements of the business so far, I fail to see a compelling risk-reward here.

data.title: Experian: Attractive Setup Given Cheap Relative Valuation And Solid Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a buy rating for Experian (OTCQX:EXPGF). The Ascend platform is well embedded in the workflow of major lenders, while the Consumer Services business has scaled into a platform with real data advantages. Add to that consistent organic growth, expanding margins, and a relative valuation sitting near multi-year lows, and I see a setup where the risk-reward looks very attractive.

Business description

EXPGF is a data and software business that helps clients manage risk and fraud and make better decisions. There are five main verticals: financial services, health, automotive, insurance, and marketing, each with its own large proprietary datasets. EXPGF also has a direct-to-consumer [consumer services] arm that helps consumers to track credit, protect identities, and handle day-to-day financial matters.

1H26 earnings

The 1H26 headline numbers were strong, with organic revenue growing by 8% y/y, landing at the top of guidance, and the sequential trend moving the right way (Q1 came in at 8% y/y, and Q2 accelerated to 9% y/y). On a constant currency basis [CCB], total revenue grew even stronger at 12% y/y. By region, North America remains the main growth driver. Organic growth saw 10% y/y in 1H, with B2B up 12% y/y and consumer sub-segment organic growth up 8% y/y. The profit performance was also very strong. Benchmark EBIT (this is EXPGF's non-GAAP measure) grew 14% y/y, with margins moving up as well, expanding 50 bps CCB and 30 bps on a reported basis. The more important metric was the 100 bps organic margin expansion, which shows us that EXPGF is benefiting from meaningful operating leverage, despite EXPGF integrating new acquisitions that are temporarily margin dilutive. This translated to a strong benchmark EPS growth of 12% y/y.

The Ascend Platform

I believe the Ascend platform has a very strong edge. This is not just any typical analytics toolkit; I see it as a core infrastructure for the financial institutions in the US and UK. To put things into perspective (per the RBC 2025 TIMT conference), more than 20 of the top 25 US financial institutions now use an Ascend module, and in the UK, about two-thirds of all lending volume depends on Ascend in some form. Clearly, this Ascend platform is part of the workflow, and given this level of integration, it becomes incredibly hard for a competitor to displace since ripping out Ascend would disrupt a significant amount of lending (too much operational risk).

Here is how I see the competitive landscape. Some competitors might offer a very strong point solution, like a single decisioning module, or sell data feeds, and these could be better than EXPGF solutions on a stand-alone basis. But they cannot compare to EXPGF solutions as a whole, one that combines data, fraud-prevention tools, etc., in one ecosystem. Take the case of a bank: Ascend becomes the operating system that helps it make credit decisions. Throughout the entire lifecycle, from building credit models to deployment and monitoring risk, the switching costs become massive.

Consumer Services

Similar to the Ascend platform, I believe EXPGF's Consumer Services business has reached a scale where it looks less like an optional credit-monitoring tool and more like a core financial platform for consumers. To give a sense of scale, there are now more than 208 million members globally, and this is a massive distribution footprint, which I expect to only get larger. The most important driver for this is the well-executed connected-data strategy. In North America, EXPGF now has 18 million connected bank accounts, and this is a huge edge vs. peers that do not have this function. Banks that are connected provide real-time visibility into income, spending patterns, and bill payments, which are important proprietary data points that a credit report doesn't provide.

As you can imagine, with more sophisticated data, EXPGF can run analytics to identify consumers with high purchase intent (for various opportunities) with precision. One good case study is the insurance marketplace. Because EXPGF can see both the consumer's current insurance provider and their renewal timing, it can easily push through a personalized quote that fits the user's needs (at the right timing). And this timing advantage is something a stand-alone competitor cannot replicate without similar data access.

Layered on top of that is the flywheel effect, which accelerates the path of scaling up. The flywheel impact is quite obvious. As a consumer gets a better offer or a more convenient experience, the corresponding partners also get higher-quality leads, and if you see this as a marketplace model, with more demand comes more supply, which attracts more demand. Underlying all these is a larger "demand/supply" liquidity pool, which opens the path for EXPNGF to attract partners from other verticals like automotive and other financial management tools.

The Data Moat

The main bear case argument for EXPGF is that AI will somehow weaken its data advantage. I disagree completely on this. My view is that AI models don't replace the value of EXPGF data; they magnify EXPGF data moat. Here is food for thought. EXPGF has one of the best datasets in the market; as mentioned above, in North America alone, it has >12,000 contributors feeding it with information, and it ingests ~1.1 billion new data points every month (source: RBC conference). That is a lot of data, and it is not something one can just use AI to scrape from the open web. This is regulated credit information that is private and collected through formal agreements.

Let me take a step further to explain why AI is not going to displace the EXPGF data moat. The important point here is that the moat isn't just the breadth of data but the underlying infrastructure. You simply cannot recreate the credit history of millions of Americans by throwing AI at the problem (where is AI going to get all the data anyway?). You need thousands of bank relationships, regulatory approvals, and a scalable working system to manage all this sensitive data at scale. At the end of the day, the quality of the AI model depends on the inputs, and the EXPGF edge here is significant. Hence, I believe instead of being bypassed by AI, the EXPGF moat has gotten a lot stronger since it can use AI to further widen the gap between itself and the next sub-scale competitor.

Valuation

Despite delivering consistent organic revenue and adj. EPS growth, EXPGF stock has de-rated significantly to levels that I find too cheap. It now trades at 23x forward PE, which is the lowest point in recent history relative to the Stoxx 600 index. I don't think there is anything fundamentally wrong with EXPGF, and hence, this relative valuation seems like a compelling entry point. My best guess is that the market is reacting to fears about AI disruption, but as I have explained above, there is nothing to worry about. If anything, AI amplifies EXPGF's moat.

For a business with products that are becoming a core utility for businesses and consumers, with consistent growth and expanding margins, paying a multi-year low relative multiple for a business doesn't seem aggressive. Once the market realizes that there is nothing to worry about with AI disruption, we should see investor confidence come back online, and coupled with continuous earnings growth, I expect a re-rating in the coming quarters. If the relative multiple goes back to the average of ~2x, this implies EXPGF forward PE to be ~29x, or 26% upside just based on multiples re-rating.

Risk

A key risk to look out for is regulatory friction. The recent CFPB lawsuit regarding consumer dispute processes is a reminder that regulators are still scrutinizing this industry. Any adverse change here could materially disrupt how EXPGF operates. FX swings are also going to continue weighing on reported results, which may trigger sell-offs on the stock as it masks the underlying momentum.

Conclusion

I recommend a buy rating for EXPGF. The competitive advantage is strong, with both Ascend and Consumer Services moving in the right direction to become core infrastructure tools rather than just simple add-ons. The market's fear of AI disruption is incorrect, in my view, as it actually strengthens EXPGF's edge. With continuous earnings growth, improving margins, and a valuation well below historical norms, I believe now is a good entry point.

data.title: Virtus KAR International Small-Mid Cap Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

The MSCI All Country World Ex USA Small Mid Cap Index advanced 6.22% during the third quarter, pushing the year-to-date return to 26.78%. Speculation around artificial intelligence (AI) has been a seemingly all-pervasive market focus in recent months. Sectors perceived to benefit from AI, like materials and information technology, performed strongly, while more defensive sectors, like consumer staples and communication services, underperformed. As a potential sign of the level of speculation in the market, unprofitable companies collectively more than doubled the return of the benchmark during the quarter.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR International Small-Mid Cap Fund returned -4.98% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the MSCI All Country World Ex USA Small Mid Cap Index. Given our focus on profitable companies with established competitive positions, the outperformance of unprofitable companies in areas like AI where the competitive landscape is shifting rapidly has been a strong headwind to performance.

Haw Par and Caixa Seguridade Participacoes were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Haw Par (OTCPK:HAWPF)(OTCPK:HAWPY) is an investment holding company listed in Singapore. The company reported solid business results and benefited from overall outperformance of Singapore equities during the quarter.
  • Caixa Seguridade Participacoes is one of the largest insurance brokers in Brazil. As we see it, the company has attractive joint venture interests in the entities that underwrite the policies Caixa distributes. Shares closed ahead in the quarter, with strong quarter-end share price performance driven by continued strength in mortgage insurance despite elevated and rising rates. Parts of Caixa Seguridade’s business remain challenged by macro headwinds, but we believe the long-term underlying business drivers that initially attracted us to the investment case remain intact.

Baltic Classifieds and Sopra Steria Group were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Listed in London, Baltic Classifieds operates several online classified portals across the Baltic states of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia. Full-year guidance was below expectations, driven by weakness in the Estonian auto market because of a new auto tax.
  • Sopra Steria (OTCPK:SPSAF) is one of the largest IT services providers in Europe. The company partners with governments, financial services companies, and aerospace and defense companies to provide technology deployments and support. The company reported weak results as the difficult demand environment weighed on Sopra’s business.

data.title: TCW Core Fixed Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Now Showing: A Cooling Labor Market

If a swift and sharp recovery from tariff-driven volatility was the surprise Spring blockbuster, then a weakening labor market – evidenced by underwhelming payroll prints and significant downward revisions – was the summer flop. Combined with other employment indicators pointing to a low-hiring, low-firing state of the job market, the possibility of a further deterioration prompted the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) to implement a “risk-management cut” with a 25 basis point (BP) reduction to the policy rate. While swaps pricing reflected a unanimous vote by market participants for the rate cut by the time the September meeting arrived, the tenor from within the FOMC over the quarter appeared to be anything but. Indeed, two Governors dissented at the July meeting (the first two-Governor dissent in decades) in favor of an earlier start to rate cuts, while others cautioned against easing in the face of still-elevated inflation. Despite the divide in opinions, preventing any further softening in the labor market was shown to be top priority with the realized rate cut and median FOMC projection of two more cuts by year end. However, there remains lingering uncertainty among some market participants about potential longer-term tariff impacts and the Federal Reserve’s (the Fed) independence that weighed on longer yields, resulting in their relative underperformance versus the front of the curve. Illustratively, 1- and 2-Year yields fell by 35 and 11 bps, respectively, while the 10- and 30-Year yields moved lower by 8 and 4 bps, respectively.

Despite the potentially troublesome developments that forced the Fed’s hand, risk assets turned in another quarter of strong returns as investors appeared to focus more on the valuation impacts of lower rates and less on the underlying reason for the rate cut. Equities once again outperformed with the S&P 500 Index gaining 8% while the tech-heavy NASDAQ Index returned more than 11%. Though more modest, fixed income markets also generated positive returns, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index finishing the quarter up 2%. Excluding the rate impact on bonds, the excess return (outperformance versus similar duration Treasuries) for the Aggregate Bond Index reached nearly 50 bps as yield spreads tightened across sectors. The strongest return profile belonged to corporate credit with investment grade and high yield issues gaining 2.6% and 2.5%, respectively, supported by robust demand. With the future path of rates coming into greater clarity over the period, investors sought to lock in corporate yields at still-attractive levels, which also prompted a wave of record supply in both investment grade and high yield markets as borrowers in turn sought to capitalize on receptive capital markets and favorable borrowing costs (falling yields, reduced spreads). Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) gained 2.4% alongside declining interest rate volatility to lead securitized products, while the non-agency residential MBS sector saw broad-based advances across collateral types as spreads moved tighter and prices increased. Commercial MBS (CMBS) gained 1.8%, with positive total returns from both agency and private label deals, though private label issues outperformed given the risk-on backdrop. Finally, Index-eligible asset-backed securities (ABS) gained 1.6%, though auto and credit card collateral underperformed non-traditional sectors like collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) which benefitted from the strong appetite for spread and yield premiums offered, especially relative to corporates of similar quality and maturity.

Performance

The TCW Core Fixed Income Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 1.98% net of fees in the third quarter of 2025 to finish five bps behind the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (“Index”), while year-to-date performance remained 22 bps ahead of the Index. For the quarter, modest underperformance was driven by the Fund’s continued underweight to investment grade corporate credit as corporate spreads continued to tightened. Meanwhile, the Fund benefitted from the broad overweight to securitized sectors, particularly residential MBS. Agency MBS rewarded relative returns given the strong performance at the sector level amid easing rate volatility, while the Fund’s emphasis on lower and belly coupons within the 30-Year coupon stack provided an additional boost from an issue selection standpoint. Though this coupon positioning was a drag early in the year when the “carry trade” dominated and premium coupons benefitted, it has shifted to a tailwind that now supports year-to-date relative outperformance amid heightened prepayment concerns that have negatively impacted premium coupon performance in favor of the total return potential of discount-priced coupons. Away from residential MBS, the overweight position in private label CMBS also contributed, with a small additional boost from the focus on single asset single borrower deal structures which have performed well year-to-date in the broader risk-on backdrop and continued sponsorship of performing properties. Finally, the duration position was trimmed alongside the decline in Treasury yields during the quarter, but remained longer-than-Index, producing a modest benefit to relative performance.

Outlook & Positioning

We entered the year with a recognition that the distribution of possible economic outcomes had become wider given the administration's penchant for volatility, with a bias towards more pessimistic possibilities. While the view remains that the economy is likely to slow from here, it has become increasingly clear that policy sequencing matters, and that the administration's more aggressive policies and attendant headwinds appear to be fading. In contrast, there are components of the administration's agenda that are pro-growth and that are likely to provide economic tailwinds in the future, but the ability of the economy to bridge the gap between then and now is made more complicated by the demonstrable weakening that has occurred in the labor market. This development in our view is the single-largest determinant for the future direction of the economy; the jobs market turning from “low-hiring, low-firing” to “no-hiring and firing” likely means the difference between a mere slowing of growth and something that more closely resembles a recession, while the speed with which the pro-growth policy can be implemented and provide economic support is also set to play a meaningful yet protracted part. Regardless of which path the economy ultimately travels, pricing across most risk assets appears to be focused on only the most optimistic of outcomes as equity markets continue to reach new highs and credit spreads remain near historical lows, providing investors minimal protection against future volatility and necessitating diligent underwriting.

As value investors, TCW remains vigilant in its assessment of risks and opportunities across markets. With current corporate spread levels presenting an asymmetric risk and reward profile, the position in corporate credit continues to represent a sizeable underweight versus the Index, with selective exposure that is more defensive in nature (up in quality, focus on non-cyclical industries, emphasis on liquidity). Securitized products, however, currently offer a more compelling relative value argument and thus represent a more attractive segment of the market in which to allocate portfolio risk budgets. Agency MBS represents the largest position given the sector's liquidity, spread premiums, and government guarantee, with exposure focused on lower and belly coupons for pull-to-par price potential and better call protection, respectively. Non-agency MBS is a large off-Index allocation, favored for robust collateral profiles across that allow for various opportunities to capture strong risk-adjusted yields. Meanwhile, CMBS exposure is focused on single asset single borrower deals, selected based on a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor that facilitates bottom-up inclusion criteria. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted positions including senior CLO tranches and high-quality non-traditional collateral types. Finally, expectations for rates to continue moving lower and for the Fed to move more quickly than anticipated by markets should the labor market weaken further informs a still longer-than-index duration position of approximately 0.2 years, and an overweight to the front-end of the curve.

GLOSSARY OF TERMS

Agency MBS – The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Asset-Backed Securities – A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Basis Point (BPS) – One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index or “the Agg” – Is a broad-based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFS) as a benchmark to measure their relative performance. Bond – A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. Call – To force an option writer to sell shares of stock at a price stipulated in a contract. Carry Trade – A strategy in which an investor sells a certain currency with a relatively low interest rate and uses the funds to purchase a different currency yielding a higher interest rate. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities) – A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations) – A special purpose vehicle with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. Collateral – Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit – A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation's debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Coupon – The interest rate stated on a bond when it's issued. The coupon is typically paid semiannually. Duration – A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. Excess Return – The rate of return on a security or an investment portfolio in excess of the return generated by a portfolio of U.S. Treasury securities with similar duration and partial durations. Federal Reserve (the Fed) – The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) – The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. Headwinds – Headwinds in an economic situation represent events or conditions e.g. a credit crisis, rising costs, natural disasters, etc, that slow down the growth of an economy. Inflation – A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Investment Grade – A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody's, Standard & Poors and Fitch. Labor Market – The nominal market in which workers find paying work, employers find willing workers, and wage rates are determined. Liquidity – The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. Nasdaq – Nasdaq is a global electronic marketplace for buying and selling securities, as well as the benchmark index for U.S. technology stocks. Nasdaq was created by the National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD) to enable investors to trade securities on a computerized, speedy and transparent system, and commenced operations on February 8, 1971. The term “Nasdaq” is also used to refer to the Nasdaq Composite, an index of more than 3,000 stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange that includes the world's foremost technology and biotech giants such as Apple, Google, Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon, Intel and Amgen. Non-agency – Mortgage backed securities made up of mortgage loans that are not guaranteed by a government-supported agency. Non-Agency MBS – Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Non-cyclical – Non-cyclical stocks repeatedly outperform the market when economic growth slows. Non-cyclical securities are generally profitable regardless of economic trends because they produce or distribute goods and services we always need, including things like food, power, water, and gas. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. Recession – Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country's gross domestic product. Risk Asset – Any asset that carries a degree of risk. S&P 500 – The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor's 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Securitized – When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Spread – The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Swaps – The most common and simplest swap, an agreement between two parties to exchange sequences of cash flows for a set period of time. Tariff – A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Total Return – The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Tranches – Are segments created from a pool of securities, usually debt instruments such as bonds or mortgages, that are divided up by risk, time to maturity, or other characteristics in order to be marketable to different investors. Each portion or tranche of a securitized or structured product is one of several related securities offered at the same time, but with varying risks, rewards and maturities to appeal to a diverse range of investors. Underperform – When an investment is

underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight – A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security's weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Valuations – The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility – A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield – The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment's cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Premium – Additional return.
